name: Button
summary: Theme-aware action button with `variant` (filled/outlined/plain), semantic `intent`, and `size`; reach for it for any primary or secondary click action, and use `IconButton` instead when the control is icon-only.

bestPractices: Establish a clear action hierarchy: use `filled` (the default) for the primary action and `outlined` or `plain` for secondary/tertiary actions. Keep emphasis consistent within a view.
Choose sizes by density: `sm` / `md` for typical forms, `lg` for standout CTAs. Avoid mixing sizes within the same control group.
Use `variant` (`filled|outlined|plain`) and semantic `intent` to establish hierarchy. For custom palettes, use `color` (token name or CSS) ā€” `filled` auto-contrasts the label.
Buttons default to `type='button'`, so they will NOT submit a form. For the submit control inside a `<form>`, set `type='submit'` explicitly; use `type='reset'` to clear fields.
Use `fullWidth` in narrow or linear layouts (mobile, drawers) where the button should span the container. Otherwise let content define width.
For a single CTA inside a column/Stack, center using a parent container (`<Box alignX='center'>`) or `fullWidth` for stretch; avoid control-level centering.
Prefer succinct verbā€‘first labels. If the action is iconā€‘only, use `IconButton` and provide an accessible label via `aria-label` .
When combining icons and text, wrap them in a row layout (e.g., `<Stack direction='row' gap={theme.spacing(1)}>`) for readable spacing rather than relying on the label alone.
Reuse styling with `preset` ( `definePreset()` ) rather than duplicating `sx` across instances.
Avoid multiple primary actions per view and reserve destructive styles for destructive operations.

examples.title: Submit and reset buttons in a form
examples.code:
<form onSubmit={(e) => e.preventDefault()}>
  <Stack direction='row' gap={theme.spacing(1)}>
    <Button type='submit'>Save</Button>
    <Button type='reset' variant='outlined'>Reset</Button>
  </Stack>
</form>
